coredata.dc:title: The enhanced oxygen evolution reaction via carbon quantum dots induced in-situ phase transition for overall seawater splitting at industrial-level current densities

coredata.dc:description: Preparing low-cost, high-performance and chlorine-repelling Fe-based catalysts is crucial for achieving industrial-level current overall seawater splitting. Herein, we develop a nickel foam supported bifunctional electrocatalyst by embedding zero-dimensional (0-D) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within amorphous FePx (denoted as a-CQDs-FePx/NF), which features multiple active sites and overall seawater splitting for enhanced catalysis activity. The optimized a-CQDs-FePx/NF delivers an overpotential of just 308 mV at 1000 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH. Furthermore, the a-CQDs-FePx/NF bifunctional catalyst delivers a voltage of 1.97 V in an alkaline seawater electrolytic cell at 1000 mA cm−2. Notably, the embedded CQDs and the in-situ formed POx-functionalized metal oxygen/hydroxide (P-FeOOH) enhanced the catalyst’s stability under a high current of 1000 mA cm−2 for 200 h in a mixture of 1 M KOH and seawater. Additionally, the CQDs greatly promote the irreversible phase transition from FePx to P-FeOOH during the OER process. This CQDs-induced strategy provides valuable insight into an idea of feasible catalyst design for industrial seawater electrolysis.

(a) XRD patterns of a-CQDs-FePx and a-FePx; (b) Raman spectra of a-CQDs-FePx and a-FePx; high-resolution XPS of (c) Fe 2p, (d) P 2p of a-CQDs-FePx and a-FePx.
(a-b) OER polarization curves in 1 M KOH and corresponding Tafel plots of different catalysts. (c-d) HER polarization curves in 1 M KOH and corresponding Tafel plots of different catalysts.

(a) Schematic diagram of a-CQDs-FePx/NF for overall seawater splitting. (b) OER and HER polarization curves of in different electrolytes. (c) a-CQDs-FePx/NF catalysts in a two-electrode electrolyzer (1 M KOH and 1 M KOH + seawater, resistance: ∼1.2 Ω).

(a) Durability tests of a-FePx/NF and a-CQDs-FePx/NF at 1000 mA cm−2 of 100 h and 200 h in 1 M KOH + seawater, respectively (Inset image: HRTEM of a-CQDs-FePx after durability tests.). In-situ Raman spectra of the a-FePx/NF (b) and a-CQDs-FePx/NF (c) catalyst at various potentials for the OER process. (d) P 2p XPS spectra of a-CQDs-FePx/NF and a-FePx/NF during chronoamperometry tests (traced per 10 min in alkaline seawater).

head.ce:title: The enhanced oxygen evolution reaction via carbon quantum dots induced in-situ phase transition for overall seawater splitting at industrial-level current densities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The embedded carbon quantum dots accelerate the phase transition in anodic reaction and enhance the active area, which make bifunctional catalyst (a-CQDs-FePx/NF) achieve overpotential of 308 mV and 325 mV in OER and HER at 1000mA cm-2, reinforce stability of a-CQDs-FePx/NF at high current density of 1000mA cm-2 in alkaline seawater electrolyte.

Preparing low-cost, high-performance and chlorine-repelling Fe-based catalysts is crucial for achieving industrial-level current overall seawater splitting. Herein, we develop a nickel foam supported bifunctional electrocatalyst by embedding zero-dimensional (0-D) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within amorphous FePx (denoted as a-CQDs-FePx/NF), which features multiple active sites and overall seawater splitting for enhanced catalysis activity. The optimized a-CQDs-FePx/NF delivers an overpotential of just 308 mV at 1000 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH. Furthermore, the a-CQDs-FePx/NF bifunctional catalyst delivers a voltage of 1.97 V in an alkaline seawater electrolytic cell at 1000 mA cm−2. Notably, the embedded CQDs and the in-situ formed POx-functionalized metal oxygen/hydroxide (P-FeOOH) enhanced the catalyst’s stability under a high current of 1000 mA cm−2 for 200 h in a mixture of 1 M KOH and seawater. Additionally, the CQDs greatly promote the irreversible phase transition from FePx to P-FeOOH during the OER process. This CQDs-induced strategy provides valuable insight into an idea of feasible catalyst design for industrial seawater electrolysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen, one of the bulk chemicals with the yield expecting to reach 179 million ton year−1 in 2030 [1–3], has received a hot-spot of scientific community due to their clean, sustainable and zero emission feature. Nowadays, the hydrogen production technologies, such as steam reforming of hydrocarbons, commonly rely on high temperature and pressure (the consumption of huge fossil energy) as well as expensive substantial infrastructure (a centralized industry) [4–6]. As a contrast, the electrochemical water splitting technology is a more facile and sustainable process, which can be achieved even in a simple electrolytic cell to obtain a high purity and be powered by the excess electric energy originating from wave, wind, and solar power on remote region [7]. However, the commonly reported electrocatalytic water splitting systems require a persistent supply of high purity water feed to maintain the high efficiency of the catalyst, which dramatically increase the cost of hydrogen production due to the scarcity of freshwater [8,9]. Direct electrolysis of seawater has greatly realized the rational allocation of resources, which is particularly important in arid areas. However, splitting of seawater, especially in anodic reaction, still faces high challenges. Oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and the chlorine evolution reaction (CER) exist at the same time at the anode, and the CER is a two-electron process, which is easier than the four electron OER reaction [10–17]. Hence, the design and fabrication of catalysts with high selectivity, sufficient active sites and robust stability, still remain the biggest challenge faced by seawater splitting.

Transition metal-based catalysts have optimistic research value in alkaline seawater electrolysis because of their low cost, wide existence, and tunable electronic structures [18–21]. In particular, the surface of transition metal-based catalyst converts to metal hydroxide (*OOH), which has been proven to be a genuine active center in the anodic reaction of alkaline electrolytes [22,23]. Accordingly, a large number of irreversible phase transitions to metal-OOH in the OER point to a more favorable catalytic reaction. Various efforts depend on phase-transition-engineering to regulate the transformation of morphology or crystalline phase have been studied either before or during the anode reaction for excellent catalytic performance [24–28]. Indeed, research focusing on amorphous transition metal-based catalysts with phase-transition-engineering during the OER process has not been developed deeply with subtle strategy.

It is worth noting that clear signatures of CQDs have been observed, such as ultra-small size, high stability, and good conductivity [29–31], moreover, the OH and COOH group can firmly combine with metal elements to form short-range ordered or amorphous phases, and adjust the intrinsic activity of catalysis [32,33]. There are different methods have been attempted to prepare integrated catalysts to combine with CQDs for electro-catalysis with remarkable progress [34–37]. The introduction of CQDs may perturb the ordered internal structure of the catalyst. This disruption can lead to the generation of more defects or irregularities on the catalyst surface [38]. Thus, the scientific introduction of CQDs paves a practicable avenue for increasing the bifunctional catalyst active sites and enhancing the overall water splitting of the catalytic reaction.

Based on the above considerations, this study focuses on the in-situ, uniform doping of 0D carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into FePx, the preparation of an amorphous a-CQDs-FePx/NF catalyst, and the evaluation of its performance in seawater electrolysis. The introduction of CQDs will rearrange the energy of alternate interfaces of different dimensions supported on Ni foam, further leading to the faster irreversible phase transition (amorphous FePx to active intermediate P-FeOOH) under external voltage, thus speeding up the reaction process of OER with an overpotential of 308 mV at 1000 mA cm−2, the alkaline seawater electrolytic cell with a-CQDs-FePx/NF as cathode and anode reaches 1.97 V at 1000 mA cm−2 current density. It is encouraging that the simple doping of quantum dots can improve the poor stability of amorphous materials under 1000 mA cm−2 current density, and still retain 94 % of the current intensity after 200 h. Furthermore, data of XPS and in-suit Raman instruments deliver ample evidence that the addition of quantum dots plays a vital role in promoting the irreversible phase transition in the OER process, and in-situ generated P-FeOOH can stable the *OOH intermediate for fast reaction kinetics. Our research proposes a strategy that the introduction of CQDs accelerates the phase transition process of the exposed active sites in catalyst and reinforces the stability of the catalytic materials, which puts forward a solution for the preparation of catalysts in the future even for industrial applications.

The preparation of carbon quantum dots is based on previous work [39]. DC voltage electrolysis: Put 1.0 g sodium hydroxide (NaOH) into 10 mL H2O. After dissolve completely, add 140 mL ethanol (C2H5OH) to get uniform electrolyte. Use two platinum sheets (1 × 1 cm2) as anode and cathode, and ensure the position of two electrodes face to face. After 24 h DC voltage electrolysis, dark brown electrolyte is obtained. Rotary distillation extraction: Mixing the brown solution with 150 mL ethanol, the dark brown solid was obtained with the evaporation of the solvent by rotary steamer. Dialysis extraction: Dissolved the brown solid in 100 mL deionized water and put it into the dialysis bag, then put the dialysis sample into the environment of deionized water, and replace the deionized water until pH = 7. CQDs with final concentration of 1 mg/mL is prepared. The whole electrolysis reaction is carried out at room temperature.

Before hydrothermal synthesis, we need to fully pre-treated the impurities on the surface of Ni foam: washing foam nickel with 4 mol/L hydrochloric acid and ethanol solution for 30 min separately. Finally, put nickel foam in the vacuum oven to dry. After pre-treated, put 35 mL of the mixed solution (1:1 ratio of CQDs and DI) into 40 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) reactor liner. After adding 0.4 g FeCl3·6H2O and 0.3 g NaSO4, the mixed solution is stirred by magnetic for 10 min, and then the treated nickel foam (2 × 3 cm2) is immersed in the mixed solution. The reactor is heated to 110 ℃ in the oven for 6 h [40]. Nickel foam is taken out until the reactor cooled down, and then washed with water and ethanol, respectively. Finally, the nickel foam loaded with nanomaterials is placed in a vacuum dryer for 6 h at 60 ℃. The same method applies to different proportions of CQDs (10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %).

The a-CQDs-FePx/NF is synthesized by conventional gas phase phosphating method: a piece of CQDs-FeOOH/Ni foam (2 × 3 cm2) is placed at the end of a tube furnace and the other end placed 1 g sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO2). Thermal phosphorization is conducted at 300 °C under a flow of 30 sccm Ar for 2 h with 2 ℃/min heating rate. a-FePx/NF is prepared by the same method without CQDs. For comparison with commercial catalysts. RuO2/NF and Pt-C/NF catalysts are prepared. 1 mg Pt/C and 1 mg RuO2 are respectively dispersed in 1 mL naphthol (Nafion 5 wt%) by sonication to form homogeneous inks with the concentration of 1 mg/mL. The dispersion is firmly coated onto a Ni foam substrate, which is dried in air overnight.

The morphology of as-obtained electrodes are characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. S1a, the surface of NF is covered by hierarchical structure consists of wrinkle nanolayer comparison of bare nickel foam surface (Fig. 1
a), belong to the crystalline planes of FeOOH (PDF#75–1594) (Fig. S1b). After phosphating process, the morphology of precursor is well maintained with some breakage around the edge of nanolayer (Fig. 1 b-c). The TEM images of a-FePx nanoparticle show irregular nanosheet structures (Fig. 1 d), the mixed crystalline phase structure contains an obvious Fe2P (111) crystal plane with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, and an amorphous phase structure with no regular crystal plane around it (Fig. 1 e). For the a-CQDs-FePx sample, there are some circular nanoparticles with the size of 5–7 nm involved in amorphous FePx (Fig. 1 f), and the HRTEM image of each circular nanoparticle presents a crystal plane with a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm corresponding to the (020) crystal plane of CQDs (Fig. 1 g), confirming the homogeneous doping of CQDs and further CQDs aggravate the disorder of the crystalline phase of FePx. In addition, the a-CQDs-FePx sample is characterized by elemental mapping technology, the well distributed Fe, P, C elements are seen from element mapping images (Fig. 1 h). Moreover, the Fe and P exhibited a distribution characteristic close to a 1:2 ratio within the sample(Fig. S3).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of catalysts are measured to explore phase and crystallinity. As shown in Fig. S1b, the XRD pattern of CQDs-FeOOH displays a series of characteristic peaks of definite FeOOH phase. After the phosphating process, the XRD pattern of a-FePx/NF shows obvious peaks of Fe2P and peaks of nickel foam. However, only the peaks of nickel foam can be observed for the a-CQDs-FePx/NF sample, indicating that the doping of CQDs can enhance the disorder of atomic arrangement to the form an amorphous phase (Fig. 2
a). Under the same treatment condition, the annealed material (CQDs-Fe2O3) prepared by thermal treatment without phosphorus source presents an obvious crystal Fe2O3 phase (Fig. S2). Compared with the Raman curve of a-FePx, the D-band (at 1357.5 cm−1) and G-band (at 1575.4 cm−1) of CQDs can be clearly observed in the Raman spectrum of a-CQDs-FePx (Fig. 2 b). The relative intensity of the “disorder” D-band to the crystalline G-band (ID/IG) for the CQDs is 0.78, which illustrates the quantum dots are successfully introduced with well crystallinity. To further explore the compositions and valences of different elements, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is performed. In the XPS full-spectrum diagram, characteristic peaks corresponding to the elements Fe, P, and C can be observed (Fig. S4a). The ratio of Fe to P demonstrates good consistency with the results obtained from EDX analysis (Table S1). From the binding energy (BE) of Fe 2p2/3 (Fig. 2 c), the peak located at 705.3 eV could be readily attributed to Fe0, which can be concluded that a part of Fe element is metallic state. It can be also observed that the Fe0 peak area of a-CQDs-FePx/NF is significantly larger than that of a-FePx. What’s more, the Fe0 2p peak of a-CQDs-FePx/NF locating at 706.1 eV shows a band-gap blue-shift of 0.8 eV compared with a-FePx/NF, indicating a strong coupling effect between FePx and CQDs. For the P element, the spin orbit peak at 129.3 eV can also support the existence of Fe-P bond, emphasizing that Fe-based metal phosphide are formed by metal bond in the process of low temperature phosphating. The two peaks at 133.4 eV and 129.3 eV reflecting the BE of P 2p1/2 and 2p3/2. The C 1 s spectrum of a-CQDs-FePx shows three peaks centered at 284.6 286.4 and 289.3 eV, which are assigned to C=C, C–O/C–C and C=C=O (Fig. S4b), respectively. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig.S5, the contact angle (wettability) of a-CQDs-FePx/NF and CQDs-FePx/NF on the NF surface with a droplet of 1 M KOH are further evaluated, a-CQDs-FePx/NF delivers a contact angle of almost 0°, which is lower than a-FePx/NF (∼17°). This signifies the superhydrophilic characteristics of the electrode. This property of the a-CQDs-FePx/NF electrocatalyst facilitates the swift separation of bubbles generated at high current densities, implying the active sites being more readily exposed of a-CQDs-FePx/NF to the solution, thereby accelerating the progress of the reaction [41].

The OER and HER properties of different catalysts are tested at room temperature (25 ℃) in 1 M KOH electrolyte. According to Nernst equation, the conversion formula of standard hydrogen electrode is referred to ERHE = EHg/HgO + 0.942, and all the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves are corrected by Gamry system. As shown in Fig. 3
a, the a-CQDs-FePx/NF presents a remarkable OER activity with the overpotential of 295 mV at 500 mA cm−2, which is obviously lower than those of a-FePx/NF (359 mV), CQDs-FeOOH/NF precursor (465 mV) and commercial RuO2/NF (518 mV). With the current density enhanced to 1000 mA cm−2, the over-potential of a-CQDs-FePx/NF only increased from 295 mV (at 500 mA cm−2) to 308 mV, indicating an excellent reaction kinetics. Then, the Tafel slopes of those catalysts are calculated and the results are shown in Fig. 3 b. It is observed that the a-CQDs-FePx/NF displays a minimum value of 57.9 mV dec-1 than 89.8 mV dec-1 of a-FePx/NF, 84.5 mV dec-1 of CQDs-FeOOH/NF and 110.8 mV dec-1 of RuO2/NF at high current density, reflecting that the involved CQDs and the amorphous structure can dramatically improve the OER reaction kinetics. The comparison of OER activity among the a-CQDs-FePx/NF catalyst and other reported non-noble metal catalysts (mainly Fe based catalysts) is listed in Table S2. Until then, the amount of CQDs is optimized to explore the influences on the structure and the catalytic (HER and OER) performance. With the increase of CQDs concentration, more CQDs embedded in the a-CQDs-FePx catalysts (Fig. S6). The LSV test result delivers that the catalytic performance (HER and OER) is firstly enhanced and then diminished with the increase of CQDs amount during the 10–70 % (proportion of carbon quantum dots in hydrothermal step) range, when the loading amount reaches 50 %, the optimal catalytic performance is extracted (Fig. S7- Fig. S8). Furthermore, the influence of the iR-correction rate (95 % and 80 %) on the catalytic performance of electrode materials was also investigated (Fig. S9).

On the other hand, the a-CQDs-FePx/NF is also an excellent catalyst for HER. LSV curves of different catalysts are depicted in Fig. 3 c. The a-CQDs-FePx/NF shows the best HER performance among all the catalysts in alkaline electrolyte. The corresponding overpotential of 300 mV is achieved at the current density of 500 mA cm−2 for the a-CQDs-FePx/NF, which is a little higher than 295 mV of commercial Pt-C/NF, and significantly lower than a-FePx/NF (372 mV) and CQDs-FeOOH/NF (393 mV). There is a crossover between two LSV curves of Pt/C and a-CQDs-FePx/NF, implying that the a-CQDs-FePx/NF possess better HER efficiency beyond 600 mA cm−2. The a-CQDs-FePx/NF exhibits the most outstanding catalytic ability with an overpotential of 325 mV at the current density of 1000 mA cm−2. The Tafel slope of a-CQDs-FePx/NF is calculated as 48.7 mV dec-1, which is lower than those of a-FePx/NF (106.2 mV dec-1), CQDs-FeOOH/NF (107.0 mV dec-1), and Pt-C/NF (212.3 mV dec-1). In sharp contrast to other catalysts, the in-situ loaded and CQDs doping strategy can dramatically enhance the kinetics of HER process. Mainly benefiting by good electrical conductivity of ultra-small size of CQDs and a large number of exposed active sites, the catalytic performance within OER and HER of a-CQDs-FePx/NF are significantly better than that of bare nickel foam (Fig. S10). The comparison of overpotentials of different catalytic electrodes under different current densities (100 mA cm−2, 500 mA cm−2, 1000 mA cm−2) in the histogram are clearly present in Fig. S11. The HER chronopotentiometry test is performed, as shown in Fig. S12a. The a-CQDs-FePx/NF electrode deliver a potential change of 41 mV after 20 h at a current density of 100 mA cm−2, which indicates the proper stability of the synthesized electrocatalysts for HER. Furthermore, the LSV after HER lasting test is also performed (Fig. S12b), and the degradation of activity can also be observed, potentially due to the dissolution of the active substance [42].

According to the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves at different scan rates (Fig. S13), the electrochemical double layer capacitances (Cdl) of a-CQDs-FePx/NF and a-FePx/NF are calculated to evaluate the electrochemical active surface areas (ECSA) of each catalyst. The a-CQDs-FePx/NF delivers a Cdl value of 231.6 mF cm−2, which is nearly 3.7 times of a-FePx/NF (62.3 mF cm−2) (Fig. S14). The result demonstrates that the involved CQDs dramatically enhance the ECSA of amorphous FePx with boosted accessible active sites for improving electrochemical performances. Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. S16b, the Turnover Frequency (TOF) of a-CQDs-FePx/NF (5.6) exceeds that of a-FePx/NF (0.89) by a factor of 6.3, which confirms that the incorporation of CQDs significantly enhances the intrinsic activity of a-CQDs-FePx/NF. Furthermore, the normalized OER polarization curve depicted in Fig. S15 also demonstrates the beneficial effect of CQDs in boosting the intrinsic activity of the catalyst. In addition, we have conducted further EIS experiments in different overpotentials and fit with appropriate electrical equivalent circuit [41,43–45]. By increasing the anode overpotential from 50 mV to 250 mV, the diameter of semicircles decreases of a-CQDs-FePx/NF, CQDs-FeOOH/NF and a-FePx/NF electrodes, which leads to increaseing in OER kinetics. Apparently, a-CQDs-FePx/NF exhibited the lowest charge transfer resistance in different overpotentials (Fig. S17), revealing that the phosphating process and the involved CQDs enhance the conductivity of electrode materials for the best charge transfer rate between catalyst and electrolyte interface. The HER improvement mechanism can be attributed to 3 points: ① The increased conductivity, as shown in Fig. S17d, the involved CQDs and phosphating step can dramatically enhance the conductivity of electrode materials; ② The promoted electrochemical active area, the a-CQDs-FePx/NF delivers a Cdl of 231.6 mF cm−2 (Fig. S14), which is superior to the 62.3 mF cm−2 of a-FePx/NF; ③ The involved P catalysis sites, as reported by literature [43], the P site serves as a proton acceptor to enhance the adsorption H during HER. To sum up, the high catalytic performance of a-CQDs-FePx/NF is attributed to the synergistic effect of amorphous FePx and CQDs.

The electrocatalytic performances of the a-CQDs-FePx/NF electrode in 1 M KOH, simulated seawater (1 M KOH + 0.5 M NaCl) and alkaline seawater (1 M KOH + seawater) are investigated. The alkaline seawater electrolyte possesses certain technological superiorities: (1) OH– anion can complex with Mg2+ and Ca2+ to form precipitation (Fig. S18), which can prevent the blocking of channels and active centers in catalysis. (2) OH– anion can kill the microorganisms in seawater to avoid the resulting interference. (3) OH– anion can enhance the OER activity to lower the overpotential. The LSV curves of different electrolytes intuitively show that the best catalytic performance obtained in the pure 1 M KOH electrolyte. After adding NaCl into KOH to form the simulated seawater electrolyte, the electro-catalytic performance of a-CQDs-FePx/NF was similar to that in pure KOH electrolyte, and the overpotential of OER controlled at 310 mV at 1000 current density. In the alkaline dielectric with actual seawater participation, the overpotential of OER and HER locate at 307 mV and 330 mV at 1000 current density, which are not significantly weakened compared with those in other electrolytes (Fig. 4
b). The comparison of overpotentials for catalysts in different electrolytes under different current densities (100 mA cm−2, 500 mA cm−2, 1000 mA cm−2) in the histogram are clearly present in Fig. S19. Performances of a-CQDs-FePx/NF catalyst in actual alkaline seawater perfectly be capable to control the scope of chlorine oxidation under high current density, so as to avoid the generation of by-products (Fig. 4 a). As the a-CQDs-FePx/NF can be used as a bifunctional catalyst at room temperature (Fig. 4 c), the overall seawater splitting performance is further investigated by two a-CQDs-FePx/NF electrodes in treated alkaline seawater, the two-electrode device is shown in Fig. S20. As shown in Fig. 4 c, a very low cell voltage of 1.45 V achieved at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 for overall water splitting, which presents remarkable advantages compared with many other bifunctional catalysts (Table S3). What’s more, large current densities of 500 mA cm−2 and 1000 mA cm−2 are reached with the cell voltages as low as 1.87 V and 1.97 V in alkaline seawater electrolyte, which are comparable with simulated seawater of 1.85 V and 1.95 V. In order to ensure oxygen production rather than chlorine in anode reaction under high current density, the electrolyte in the reaction (simulated seawater and actual seawater) is detected by using DPD reagent. As shown in Fig. S21 that the color of each electrolyte after the reaction is still consistent with the sample solution, which proved no chlorine produced in OER process.

In order to evaluate the practicability of a-CQDs-FePx/NF, the long-term stability operated at high current density in a two-electrode alkaline seawater system by the chronoamperometry method. The 24-hour stability of a-CQDs-FePx/NF is tested at 100 mA cm−2, the curve is very flat and the current has no downward trend (Fig. S22). In addition, the Faraday efficiency (FE) for OER under 100 mA cm−2 has been detected by gas chromatography technology, the FE calculated by actual detection (99.3 %) is extremely close to 100 %, which further confirms that the anodic reaction is entirely OER rather than CER (Fig. S23). Additionally, the stability and Cl2 selectivity of the a-FePx/NF and a-CQDs-FePx/NF is compared to prove the indispensability of carbon quantum dots. As shown in Fig. 5
a, the current density is well maintained around 1000 mA cm−2 within 200 h, indicating excellent long-term electrochemical durability even under alkaline seawater environment. On the contrary, the catalytic electrode (a-FePx/NF) without CQDs decays obviously within 100 h. Meanwhile, the stability of a-CQDs-FePx/NF at 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 mA cm−2 are also tested, whenever the current densities changed, the corresponding potentials responded quickly with superior stability (Fig. S24). The presence of Cl2 and ClO- species is ascertained through iodometry, serving as an indicator of whether Cl has undergone oxidation post-electrolysis. As depicted in Fig. S25, solution I exhibited no discernible color change, implying the absence of both Cl2 and ClO- species in the solution. In stark contrast, solution II turned purple upon the introduction of the starch indicator, serving as evidence for the presence of I2, which has been oxidized by ClO- species in the solution.

The morphology after the stability reaction is exhibited in Fig. S26 and Fig. S27, the surface changes slightly under the impact of large current for a long time. Surprisingly, whether cathode or anode, quantum dots are still vividly existed in catalyst with the clear characteristic lattice (100) after long-term stability test. For cathode, the Fe 2p and P 2p spectra after stability test are shown in Fig. S28, the stretching vibration peaks of Fe0 and P0 are been exposed at 705.4 eV and 129.2 eV with slight enhancement, which demonstrating that Fe-P bond still remains involving the reduction process proceeded smoothly of cathode. For the anode, it can be analyzed from Fig. S29 that the attribution peak of Fe-P completely disappeared and Fe-O binding peaks instead the Fe-P species, after suffering from 200 h of a-CQDs-FePx/NF. Meanwhile, the peak corresponding to P0 of P 2p also disappeared, and the peaks assigned to the P-O bond are enhanced. The results demonstrate that the POx functioned FeOOH phase may be formed on the surface of a-CQDs-FePx after a long-term durability measurement of high current density. To sum up, excellent long-term stability also means that CQDs significantly make up for the poor stability of amorphous FePx.

Transition metal phosphides (TMPs) are promising electrocatalysts for water splitting refer to their high conductivity and the high electronegativity of P element. As an effective participant, the catalytic mechanism of FePx in the process of cathode for hydrogen production can be traced by previous literatures [46–48]. For the harsh OER environment, the oxidization of P will be occurred and the phase transfer reaction to form thermodynamically layered double hydroxides (LDH) should also spontaneous generation. In order to further study the surface phase evolution of FePx during the OER process, in-situ Raman spectroscopy is used to characterize the catalysts under different potentials. When the initial voltage is 0 V, the bands between 720 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1 may originate from the P-O bond of phosphate [49]. The peak at 320 cm−1 is assigned to the Fe-O vibrations belong to small amount of chemical bonded Fe phosphate species [50,51]. When the potential increases to 1.4 V, new peak appear at 551 cm−1 for the a-FePx and a-CQDs-FePx, owing to the formation of active specie Fe-OOH [52,53]. Obviously, for a-CQDs-FePx, the intensity of 55.1 cm−1 band appears sharper with the increase of voltage. In addition, the surface atomic valence state of a-CQDs-FePx and a-FePx are also explored at different OER times (per 10 min). As shown in Fig. 5c and 5d, both of the initial a-CQDs-FePx and a-FePx display obvious peaks of zero-valence P element, but the intensity of P0 peaks are gradually decreased with the increasing of testing time. The P0 peak of a-CQDs-FePx is completely disappeared at 40 min, which is consistent with the reported surface oxidation of the catalyst under harsh oxidation conditions to form related POx - anions doped Fe-LDH (POx --Fe-LDH). The nanosheet structure of POx --Fe-LDH can be distinctly observed in TEM image, which displays a thickness of several nanometers (Inset image of Fig. 5 a). For the a-FePx electrode, the vanishing rate of P0 peaks is slower than a-CQDs-FePx, implying that the involved CQDs is benefit for the permeation and substitution of OH– anions. In other words, the OER is the process of OH– continuously migrating to the surface of Fe site [54,55], and the H-proton of the hydroxide groups of Fe-LDH host can be deprotonate by the strong base OH– anions to form the FeOOH·H2O or FeOOH. Therefore, the POx-functioned metal oxygen/hydroxide (P-FeOOH) formed in the reaction process is considered to be the main active substance of catalytic activity in the OER process. Simultaneously, the spin orbital peak changes of Fe 2p of a-FePx/NF and a-CQDs-FePx/NF are been exhibited in Fig. S29 and Fig. S30. For a-FePx/NF, the Fe0 peak moves forward from 710.05 eV with the increasing time, which proves that in the continuous oxidation process of anode and Fe0 on FePx surface is gradually transformed into FeOOH. The adding CQDs accelerate this key step for a-CQDs-FePx/NF shifts from 710.14 eV positively to higher binding energy, confirming a faster phase transfer process of surface Fe from a-CQDs-FePx to FeOOH during OER activation [56,57]. Integrating of above discussion and other following the reported studies [58,59], the active species of the anode electrode should be in-situ generated POx-functioned metal oxygen/hydroxide (P-FeOOH) nanosheets, which can not only provide rich active sites during OER electrocatalysis but also stable the *OOH intermediate for fast reaction kinetics [60]. The phase transition process of active substance of the catalyst (Eqs. (1)–(2)) and the four electrons transfer process for anode (3) are as follows:
(1) Fe + 2OH– → Fe(OH)2 + 2e-
(2) Fe(OH)2 + OH–→ FeOOH + H2O + e-
(3) FeOOH + 4OH-→ FeOOH + O2 + 2H2O + 4e-

In this study, the composite of amorphous iron phosphide embedded with carbon quantum dots has been prepared with low-cost raw compositions. In alkaline electrolyte, when the current density reached 1000 mA cm−2, the overpotentials of OER and HER were 308 mV and 325 mV respectively. For the a-CQDs-FePx/NF, the strong electron transfer ability of carbon quantum dots and the advantages of amorphous phosphide can promote the conductivity and expose more active sites, which can effectively improve HER and OER performances. Moreover, adding an appropriate amount of carbon quantum dots improved the seawater resistance of the catalyst at high current density and avoided the corrosion and toxicity of seawater. More importantly, the phase transition process of active intermediates (FePx to FeOOH) is significantly accelerated by involved CQDs, providing a new design concept for the preparation of cost-saving bifunctional catalysts with excellent performance in the field of seawater splitting.
